title: Spaghetti alla puttanesca
subtitle: The sharp, salty Neapolitan sauce of tomato, olive, caper, and anchovy
excerpt: Spaghetti alla puttanesca is bold, sharp, and finished in the time it takes the pasta to cook. The sauce is tinned tomatoes cooked down with olives, capers, and anchovies, everything deeply savoury and slightly briny. The anchovies dissolve into the sauce and there is nothing fishy about it.
sourceNotes:
• Sandro Petti of the Ischia restaurant Rangio Fellone is credited with inventing or popularising the dish in the 1950s, though the story varies with the teller. The earliest printed recipe is generally traced to Italian food writing of the 1960s. No public-domain source predates the dish.
• Synthesis draws on Marcella Hazan (*Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking*, 1992) and the description in Anna Del Conte (*Gastronomy of Italy*, 1987) of the Neapolitan tradition of bold, pantry-driven tomato sauces.

content.text: is made entirely from tins and jars, cooked in the time it takes the pasta to boil. The anchovy fillets are the secret: they dissolve into the oil and leave no trace of fishiness, only a deep savouriness underneath the sharp tomato, briny olive, and acidic caper. People who say they don't like anchovies eat this without complaint.

Don't rinse the capers. Don't pit the olives yourself when pitted ones from a jar work as well here. The sauce is meant to be bold and slightly rough at the edges. Cook it fast, salt the pasta water generously, and eat it immediately, puttanesca waits for no one.
Note that the sauce is assertive enough that extra salt is rarely needed, the anchovies, olives, and capers between them provide it. Taste before adding any.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{anchovies}} of anchovy fillets and {{garlic}} of sliced garlic. Cook for 2 minutes, pressing the anchovies with a spoon. They will dissolve into the oil, leaving nothing visible. Add {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes and stir for 30 seconds.

Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, breaking them up with the spoon. Add {{olives-black}} of chopped olives and {{capers}} of capers. Stir and cook over a medium-high heat for 10 to 12 minutes until the sauce is thick. Start the pasta water while the sauce cooks.

Bring a large saucepan of water to a boil. Salt well. Cook {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti until 1 minute before done. Reserve a mug of pasta water. Drain. Toss the pasta in the sauce pan over a medium heat, adding a splash of pasta water if needed to loosen. Serve at once, with chopped parsley if using. No cheese.

items.fix: Don't salt the pasta water as heavily as usual. Taste the sauce before adding any seasoning. If it's already salty enough, the pasta water that transfers in during tossing provides the only additional salt needed.
items.cause: The anchovies, olives, and capers each contribute salt and the combination is more than the sum of the parts.

items.fix: Cook the anchovies in the oil for the full 2 minutes, pressing actively with the spoon. They should leave no visible trace by the time the tomatoes go in.
items.cause: The anchovies were not cooked long enough to dissolve into the oil.
items.symptom: Anchovy taste is pronounced and the sauce tastes fishy

items.fix: Continue simmering uncovered until the sauce is visibly thick and a spoon dragged across the pan leaves a clear trail. This is where the flavour concentrates.
items.symptom: Sauce is watery and doesn't coat the pasta

items.fix: Add the olives at the same time as the tomatoes. They soften but retain some texture over 10 minutes. Adding them earlier than this tends to turn them to paste.
items.cause: Added too early and cooked too long.

content.text: Puttanesca came from Naples and has been part of the southern Italian table since at least the 1950s. The story most often told is that it was invented out of necessity, a few tins, whatever was in the jar, and the ability to make something forceful out of very little. Whether that origin story is accurate is not really the point. The dish works because the boldness of each ingredient is the right match for the others: the acid of the tomato, the brine of olive and caper, and the depth of the anchovy form a sauce that is more coherent than its randomness suggests.
